title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: Credit Prices and Trading Volume Climb Amidst Clean Fuels Regulation Changes on the Horizon
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(09thâ€“ 15th September)
  • A total of 297,885 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a 1.51x times rise week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades recorded a fall of 26.90% as 514,385 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price increased further this week to $55.69/MTâ€¯for Type 1â€¯transfers, recording a 1.00% rise WoW; All Non-Zero transfers fell by 5.95% in prices.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price decreased by 0.33% WoW to reach $56.94/MT last week; on 17th Sep, it was $58.33/MT.
  • In the RFS market,D3 RIN prices declined by 0.30% WoW, while D4, D5, and D6 RIN prices each fell by 1.69% WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

LCFS market continues to display bullish sentiments recording a rise in average LCFS spot credit price and Type 1 transfers. The market's interest was piqued by CARB's proposal to increase the CI reduction target to 9% for 2025, up from the original 5%. The average LCFS spot credit price reached $58.33/MT on 17th Sep, with Type 1 transfer prices rising 1.00% WoW to $55.69/MT. The Type 1 trades in the market saw a rise, with 297,885 credits exchanged, marking a 1.51x increase WoW. The bipartisan group of U.S. Representatives is urging the Treasury to expedite the issuance of final guidance for the Clean Fuel Production Credit (45Z) before its 1st Jan deadline. They specifically call for the credit restricted to renewable fuels made from domestic feedstocks by domestic producers, aiming to bolster American energy independence. On the other hand, Clean Fuels Alliance America requests the Treasury to issue guidance, including safe harbor provisions, by 1st Sep. They are asking for clarity on the ability to use existing carbon life-cycle assessments for credit calculations until a final rule is in effect, guidance on climate-smart agricultural practices, and reliable methods for credit value calculation. If implemented, the timely intimation by the authorities could significantly impact U.S. clean fuel producers. They would provide certainty for planning, potentially boost the domestic biofuel industry, and support U.S. farmers. They could also ensure a smoother transition from the current blender credit to the new producer credit, preventing disruptions in production. CARB concluded its public comment analysis on the proposed amendments on 27th August, which cCarbon has thoroughly reviewed. In Oregon, the Department of Environmental Quality (DEQ) extended the deadline for public comments on the Climate Protection Program (CPP) from 30th August to 27th September. Similarly, the Washington Department of Ecology has set a deadline of 3rd October for public comments regarding draft changes in the Clean Fuel Standard (CFS) rulemaking. cCarbon will further analyze public sentiments surrounding these proposed rule changes as the deadlines approach.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_587877" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 1.00% WoW rise from $55.14 to $55.69 last week. Additionally, all non-zero credit prices fell by 5.95% compared to the previous week. Overall trade volume decreased last week. Type 1 trades saw 295,885 credits exchanged, bouncing from last week's 42.8% decline by recording a 1.51x WoW rise this week, but the total transaction volume for All Non-Zero Trades continued to fall by 26.90% WoW this week, to 514,385 credits.

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_587878" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices decreased by 0.33% WoW, reaching $56.94/MT. On 17th Sep, the average spot price was $58.33/MT, which significantly rose from $48.17/MT, the lowest point in August. The benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2024, stood at $59.56/MT as of 17th Sep, up from $57.67/MT as of 10thÂ Sep.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_587879" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 52 trades were reported in the market, with three high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was at $85.00 for 15,000 credits of Type 2, and the lowest transfer by price last week was at $43.00 per credit of Type 2, reported at a volume of 5,000 credits. The largest volume transfers of 70,000 credits in Type 1 were undertaken for prices of $57.50/MT and $57.75/MT, and the smallest volume transfers of 300 credits, a Type 1 category, were undertaken for a price of $47.00/MT.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_587880" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] Trading activity in August experienced a notable decline, with volumes falling to 1.90 MMT from July's robust 5.21 MMT. While July had witnessed a resurgence in trading to levels reminiscent of April, August saw an uptick in Type 1 prices, which had remained relatively steady during June and July. This upward price trajectory has persisted into the first two weeks of September. The suggested enhancement of the CI reduction target to 9% for 2025, a significant increase from the initial 5% annual goal, could potentially reinvigorate stakeholder engagement in the market.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_587881" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 09th â€“ 15th Sep, total VPT recorded a 9.94% fall (WoW), with all non-zero prices falling over the previous week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices declined by 0.30% WoW, while D4, D5, and D6 RIN prices each fell by 1.69% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • Methanex Corporation Signs Definitive Agreement to Acquire OCI Globalâ€™s International Methanol Business for $2.05 Billion. Methanex Corporation (â€œMethanexâ€ or the â€œCompanyâ€) (TSX:MX) (NASDAQ:MEOH) announced that it has entered into a definitive agreement to acquire OCI Globalâ€™s (â€œOCIâ€) international methanol business for $2.05 billion. The transaction includes OCIâ€™s interest in two world-scale methanol facilities in Beaumont, Texas, one of which also produces ammonia. The transaction also includes a low-carbon methanol production and marketing business and a currently idled methanol facility in the Netherlands.
  • Splitwaters to Develop E-Fuels Facilities Using Topsoe's Technologies. Splitwaters announces plans to develop a few e-fuels facilities utilizing its state-of-the-art alkaline electrolyzers and Topsoe technology for the backend fuels and chemicals production. By leveraging modular design principles and managing the projects Splitwaters aims to streamline processes and potentially reduce CAPEX by up to 30%.
  • Growth Energy Files Two Petitions for Rehearing on Refinery Exemption Decisions. This week, Growth Energy, the nationâ€™s largest biofuel trade association, filed two petitions for rehearing with the U.S. Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit regarding the Courtâ€™s decision â€“ unsealed in August â€“ on small refinery exemptions (SREs) under the Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS).
  • Mann, Kaptur, Colleagues Demand U.S. Treasury to Prioritize Domestic Energy Producers. U.S. Representatives Tracey Mann (KS-01) and Marcy Kaptur (OH-09) led 39 of their colleagues in sending a bipartisan letter to the U.S. Department of Treasury urging it to expedite the issuance of final guidance for the Clean Fuel Production Credit in advance of its January 1 deadline and to restrict the eligibility to renewable fuels made from domestic feedstocks by domestic fuel producers. Once implemented, the credit, as intended by Congress, will bolster American energy independence by prioritizing American producers and the production of biofuels made with domestically produced feedstocks.
